content.problemStatement:
Ada's classroom contains N⋅MN \cdot MN⋅M tables distributed in a grid with NNN rows and MMM columns. Each table is occupied by exactly one student.

Before starting the class, the teacher decided to shuffle the students a bit. After the shuffling, each table should be occupied by exactly one student again. In addition, each student should occupy a table that is adjacent to that student's original table, i.e. immediately to the left, right, top or bottom of that table.

Is it possible for the students to shuffle while satisfying all conditions of the teacher?
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers N and M denoting the number of rows and columns of the classroom grid.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if it is possible for every student to move to an adjacent table while keeping exactly one student per table, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: 3×3 has 9 cells (odd), so black and white counts differ – impossible. 4×4 has 16 cells (even), so a perfect shuffle exists.
2×2 (4 cells) even → YES. 2×3 (6 cells) even → YES. 5×7 (35 cells) odd → NO.

solution.approach: The classroom grid is a bipartite graph: cells can be colored black and white like a chessboard, and every adjacency connects opposite colors. After the shuffle each student moves to a cell of opposite color, therefore the number of black cells must equal the number of white cells. In an N×M rectangle the counts are equal exactly when N·M is even. Hence a valid shuffle exists iff N·M is even. The algorithm simply checks the parity of the product for each test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.
